questions.question: Which of the following is mentioned as a simple way to organize personal tasks in Notion?

options.text: A checklist divided into columns
options.explanation: The article suggests creating a simple checklist categorized into columns as an easy way to organize tasks.

options.text: A spreadsheet imported from Google Sheets
options.explanation: While Notion can embed spreadsheets, the article does not mention this as a simple task‑organizing method.

options.text: A series of email reminders
options.explanation: Email reminders are not discussed in the guide as a Notion task‑organizing technique.

options.text: A timeline view of all tasks
options.explanation: Timeline view is a possible view type, but the article highlights a checklist in columns as the simple option.

questions.question: Where can you find pre‑built personal‑task templates to use in Notion?

options.text: In the Template Gallery or the template picker
options.explanation: The guide explicitly points readers to the template gallery and the template picker for pre‑built task pages.

options.text: On the YouTube video linked in the article
options.explanation: The YouTube video demonstrates the guide but does not host the templates themselves.

options.text: Inside Notion Settings under ‘Advanced’
options.explanation: Templates are not stored in the Settings menu; they are accessed via the gallery/picker.

options.text: Through a third‑party browser extension
options.explanation: The article does not mention any browser extensions for obtaining templates.

questions.question: How can you display the same task data in different formats such as calendar, list, or table?

options.text: Create separate databases for each view
options.explanation: Duplicating databases is unnecessary; Notion lets you switch views on a single database.

options.text: Apply different views (calendar, list, table) to the same database
options.explanation: The article notes that you can apply different views to your database to show the same data in various layouts.

options.text: Export the database to CSV and re‑import it each time
options.explanation: Exporting and re‑importing would be cumbersome and is not suggested for view changes.

options.text: Change the page font to alter the layout
options.explanation: Font changes affect text appearance only, not the structural view of a database.

questions.question: According to the guide, what kind of properties can you add to each individual task page?

options.text: Only a text description
options.explanation: Notion allows far more than just a text description for tasks.

options.text: Only an image attachment
options.explanation: Images can be added, but they are not the only property type allowed.

options.text: Any properties you want, such as status, tags, or priority
options.explanation: The guide explicitly says each task can have all the properties and detailed information you want.

options.text: Only a due‑date field
options.explanation: Due dates are common, but the guide encourages adding multiple custom properties.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of archiving completed tasks in Notion?

options.text: It permanently deletes them from your workspace
options.explanation: Archiving does not delete; it merely hides them while preserving the record.

options.text: It hides them from the active view while keeping a history for reference
options.explanation: Archiving lets you keep a record of completed work without cluttering your active task list.

options.text: It moves them to a different Notion workspace automatically
options.explanation: Archiving stays within the same workspace; it does not relocate items.

options.text: It changes the task’s color to gray
options.explanation: Color changes are a formatting option, not the purpose of archiving.

questions.question: If you want to group your tasks by priority level, which Notion feature should you use?

options.text: Filters
options.explanation: Filters limit which items appear, but they don’t create visual groups.

options.text: Grouping in a view
options.explanation: The guide mentions grouping tasks (by status, tags, or priority) using view settings.

options.text: Page comments
options.explanation: Comments are for discussion, not for organizing tasks by priority.

options.text: Inline equations
options.explanation: Equations have no relevance to task grouping.

questions.question: You have a tasks database with the properties: Status (Select), Tags (Multi‑select), Due Date (Date). You need a view that shows only tasks due this week, groups them by Status, and sorts them so the soonest due date appears at the top. Which combination of Notion features must you configure?

options.text: Set a filter for Due Date, enable grouping by Status, and add a sort on Due Date descending
options.explanation: Sorting descending would place the latest dates first, not the soonest.

options.text: Apply a filter for Due Date, enable grouping by Status, and sort by Due Date ascending
options.explanation: Filtering to this week narrows the list, grouping by Status organizes them, and ascending sort shows the nearest due dates first, matching the requirement.

options.text: Only enable grouping by Status and filter for this week
options.explanation: Without sorting, the order of tasks would be arbitrary and might not show the soonest dates at the top.

options.text: Only set a filter for this week and sort by Due Date ascending
options.explanation: Without grouping, tasks would not be visually separated by priority status.
